title: Acute Spinal Cord Injury
chief_complaint: Diving accident in shallow water. Hit head on bottom of pool. Unable to move arms or legs. Complains of severe neck pain.

physical_exam.Neurologic: C5 ASIA A (complete): Motor: No motor function below C5 (C5 elbow flexion 2/5, C6-T1 0/5). No motor in lower extremities. Sensory: No sensation below C5 dermatome. No sacral sparing. Absent anal contraction and perianal sensation. Priapism present (pathognomonic for complete SCI).
physical_exam.Respiratory: Diaphragmatic breathing only (C3-5 phrenic nerve intact). No intercostal muscle function. Paradoxical chest movement.
physical_exam.Spine: Tenderness at C5-C6 spinous processes. Step-off palpable.

hidden_data.ct_spine: Burst fracture C5 vertebral body with retropulsion of fragments into spinal canal. Bilateral jumped facets C5-C6. Canal compromise 80%.
hidden_data.mri_spine: Spinal cord contusion and edema from C4-C7. Cord transection not present (anatomic but not physiologic). Ligamentous injury posterior longitudinal ligament.
hidden_data.cxr: Atelectasis from diaphragmatic breathing. Clear lungs.

correct_diagnosis: Acute Traumatic Spinal Cord Injury - C5 ASIA A (Complete) from C5 Burst Fracture with Bilateral Jumped Facets
key_learning_points: Spinal shock = loss of all spinal cord function below injury level (flaccid paralysis, areflexia, loss of autonomic function). Lasts days to weeks. Neurogenic shock = loss of sympathetic tone (T1-L2) → hypotension + bradycardia (unopposed vagal tone). Treat with fluids + vasopressors (Phenylephrine or Norepinephrine - MAP goal >85)

ASIA classification: A = Complete (no motor or sensory in S4-S5). B = Sensory incomplete (sensation in S4-S5 but no motor). C = Motor incomplete (<3/5 below level). D = Motor incomplete (≥3/5 below level). E = Normal. Sacral sparing (perianal sensation, anal contraction) = incomplete injury (BETTER prognosis)

C5 level: Motor: Elbow flexors (C5), no wrist extension (C6), no elbow extension (C7). Functional: Ventilator-dependent for life (C3-5 phrenic intact, but intercostals paralyzed). Power wheelchair with hand controls

Treatment: 1) ATLS + spinal precautions (cervical collar, log-roll). 2) MAP augmentation: Target MAP 85-90 for 7 days (improves spinal cord perfusion). Norepinephrine or Phenylephrine. 3) Steroids: CONTROVERSIAL. NASCIS trials showed modest benefit if Methylprednisolone 30mg/kg bolus + 5.4mg/kg/hr ×23h (if <3h from injury). Higher rates of sepsis, pneumonia, and mortality. Many centers no longer use. 4) Early surgical decompression (<24h) improves neurologic recovery. 5) DVT prophylaxis (LMWH + SCDs - very high risk)

Complications: Respiratory failure (ventilator-dependent), Pressure ulcers, DVT/PE, Autonomic dysreflexia (T6 and above - severe HTN from noxious stimuli below level of injury), Spasticity, Heterotopic ossification, Urinary tract infections, Bowel impaction, Depression. Rehabilitation: Acute inpatient rehab → long-term care. Life expectancy reduced but can live decades with good care
